Oskar Allerslev

Quantitative Infrastructure & Actuarial Engineering

MSc Actuarial Mathematics student at UCPH and Student Actuary at PFA Pension. I specialize in quantitative risk management (QRM), stochastic calculus, and machine learning. My focus is translating advanced mathematical theory into scalable, low-latency pricing engines and infrastructure using C++, Rust, and R.

Download CV (PDF) LinkedIn

Core Tech Stack

Rust C++ R Python SQL XGBoost

Featured Infrastructure

First Rust API (Stock VaR)

A high-performance backend built in Rust using Axum. Fetches ticker data and calculates Historical Value at Risk (VaR) based on continuous returns and empirical quantiles.

Request URL
Rust Axum API

Rough Volatility Monte Carlo Pricer

Advanced derivative pricing framework based on fractional Brownian motions (fBM). Solves Stochastic Differential Equations (SDEs) to capture the roughness of electricity spot prices and financial markets.

C++ Engine: Methodology & Implementation

The underlying Monte Carlo engine is a high-performance C++ application designed for pricing derivatives under rough stochastic volatility models, such as the Rough Bergomi model. It leverages fractional Brownian motion (fBM) to capture empirically observed volatility dynamics.

Coupled SDE System

The engine simulates the coupled Stochastic Differential Equations for the asset price StS_tSt​ and its variance VtV_tVt​:

dSt=μStdt+VtStdWt1dS_t = \mu S_t dt + \sqrt{V_t} S_t dW_t^1dSt​=μSt​dt+Vt​​St​dWt1​
Vt=V0exp⁡(ηBH(t)−12η2t2H)V_t = V_0 \exp\left( \eta B_H(t) - \frac{1}{2} \eta^2 t^{2H} \right)Vt​=V0​exp(ηBH​(t)−21​η2t2H)
fBM Simulation for H < 0.5

Simulating the fractional Brownian motion BH(t)B_H(t)BH​(t) for a Hurst parameter H<0.5H < 0.5H<0.5 requires careful consideration of its auto-covariance structure. The covariance matrix Σ\SigmaΣ for a time grid t1,t2,…,tnt_1, t_2, \dots, t_nt1​,t2​,…,tn​ is given by:

Σij=E[BH(ti)BH(tj)]=12(ti2H+tj2H−∣ti−tj∣2H)\Sigma_{ij} = \mathbb{E}[B_H(t_i)B_H(t_j)] = \frac{1}{2}(t_i^{2H} + t_j^{2H} - |t_i - t_j|^{2H})Σij​=E[BH​(ti​)BH​(tj​)]=21​(ti2H​+tj2H​−∣ti​−tj​∣2H)

To generate valid paths, the engine performs an exact Cholesky decomposition of this dense covariance matrix, Σ=LLT\Sigma = LL^TΣ=LLT. Correlated random variates are then generated by transforming a vector of independent standard normal variables ZZZ via X=LZX = LZX=LZ. This method is computationally intensive but ensures the statistical integrity of the generated rough volatility paths.
C++ Math SDEs fBM

Nitor Energy Forecasting Competition 2026

Predictive machine learning pipeline forecasting highly volatile energy spot prices across 6 distinct markets. Optimized for RMSE utilizing specialized XGBoost models and domain-specific meteorological features.

R tidymodels XGBoost Machine Learning

Lifepack (R Package)

Comprehensive R package for life insurance mathematics. Implements robust object-oriented programming (OOP), unit testing, and full roxygen2 documentation for production use.

> library(lifepack)
> Lambda <- function(x) matrix(c(-0.1, 0.1, 0.05, -0.05), 2, 2)
> R <- function(x, mu) matrix(c(mu, 0, 0, mu), 2, 2)
> dR <- function(x, mu) matrix(c(0.1, 0, 0, 0.1), 2, 2)
> equiv_premium(0, 80, Lambda, R, dR, 0.05, 0.03, 100)
[1] 0.04512
View on CRAN
R devtools OOP Unit Testing
